title: Reusable Front-End Layout & Page Patterns
description: Enumerable catalog of page layouts, content patterns, navigation patterns, and responsive-design patterns used in modern web UI.

entries.name: Hero Section
entries.summary: A large, prominent banner at the top of a page containing a headline, supporting text, a primary call-to-action, and often a background image or product visual.
entries.significance: Sets first impression and communicates value proposition within the first few seconds; the highest-visibility real estate on a landing page, so it carries the primary conversion goal.
key_facts: Typically the first full viewport a visitor sees; carries the page's main headline and primary CTA.
Common variants: centered text, split (text left / image right), full-bleed background image or video, and gradient/illustration backgrounds.
Best practice pairs one clear value-proposition headline with a single dominant CTA to avoid choice overload.
The 'above the fold' concept motivates hero content but modern research shows users do scroll, so critical content need not all fit one viewport.

entries.name: Split-Screen Layout
entries.summary: A layout that divides the viewport into two roughly equal vertical halves, each holding distinct content (e.g. text on one side, imagery on the other, or two competing choices).
entries.significance: Creates visual balance and lets two ideas compete equally for attention; effective for dual calls-to-action, before/after comparisons, or pairing a value statement with a supporting visual.
key_facts: Usually implemented as two 50/50 columns via CSS Grid or Flexbox.
Common on hero sections pairing a headline block with a product image.
On narrow viewports the two halves typically stack vertically (column-drop behavior).
Works well when both halves have equal informational weight; poorly when one side dominates.

entries.name: Bento Box Grid
entries.summary: A modular grid of variously-sized rectangular cells (like a Japanese bento lunchbox) where each cell showcases a distinct feature, image, or metric.
entries.significance: Lets a page present many pieces of content at different importance levels in one compact, scannable composition; became a dominant 2023-2024 web/UI trend, popularized by Apple's product and OS marketing.
key_facts: Named after the compartmentalized Japanese bento lunchbox.
Identified as a leading web/UI design trend for 2024.
Popularized in mainstream use by Apple across product pages and iOS/macOS marketing.
Cells vary in size to imply hierarchy; implemented with CSS Grid using spanning columns/rows.

sources.title: Revolutionizing UI/UX with the Bento UI Grid Design Trend

entries.name: Card Grid
entries.summary: A repeating grid of uniform card components, each bundling an image, title, and short description, used to present a collection of comparable items.
entries.significance: The default pattern for browsable collections (products, articles, profiles) because equal-sized cards imply equal importance and reflow cleanly across breakpoints.
key_facts: Cards act as self-contained content containers grouping related media, text, and actions.
Typically laid out with CSS Grid using repeat(auto-fill/auto-fit, minmax()) so column count adapts to viewport.
Uniform card size signals items are peers of equal importance.
Contrast with masonry: card grids keep rows aligned, masonry does not.

sources.title: Relationship of grid layout to other layout methods

entries.name: Masonry Layout
entries.summary: A layout where one axis is a strict grid (usually columns) while items on the other axis stack to fill gaps, so items of differing height pack tightly like bricks in a wall.
entries.significance: Ideal for galleries of mixed-aspect-ratio media where fixed row heights would waste space; long the signature Pinterest look, historically requiring JS libraries and now moving toward native CSS.
key_facts: One axis follows a strict grid; the other lets shorter items rise to fill gaps rather than leaving aligned rows.
Historically implemented with JavaScript libraries (e.g. Masonry.js) because CSS could not do it natively.
A native CSS masonry specification is in progress within the CSS Grid Layout spec, with competing proposals under discussion in the CSS Working Group.
As of the mid-2020s native support remained experimental/behind flags in browsers.

sources.title: Native CSS Masonry Layout In CSS Grid

entries.name: Holy Grail Layout
entries.summary: A classic full-page layout with a header, a footer, and a central row of three columns: left sidebar, main content, and right sidebar.
entries.significance: Long considered the 'holy grail' because achieving equal-height columns, a fluid center, and source-order independence was notoriously hard before modern CSS; CSS Grid now expresses it in a few lines.
key_facts: Structure: header on top, footer on bottom, three columns (left nav, main, right aside) in between.
Named for the historical difficulty of implementing it with floats/tables while keeping equal heights and a fluid center.
With CSS Grid it collapses to roughly grid-template: auto 1fr auto / auto 1fr auto plus named grid-template-areas.
grid-template-areas lets the visual layout be rearranged responsively independent of DOM source order.
examples: web app shells with dual sidebars

sources.title: Holy grail layout (Ten modern layouts pattern)
The Holy Grail Layout with CSS Grid

entries.name: Magazine / Editorial Layout
entries.summary: A print-inspired layout using a strong multi-column grid, varied type scale, pull quotes, and mixed image sizes to create dynamic, hierarchical article pages.
entries.significance: Brings the visual richness and clear hierarchy of print journalism to the web; ideal for content-heavy publications where reading flow and typographic contrast matter.
key_facts: Draws directly on Swiss/International Typographic Style grid traditions.
Uses a modular column grid to place headlines, body columns, pull quotes, and images asymmetrically.
Relies on a wide type scale and generous whitespace to establish reading hierarchy.
Often combines a strict underlying grid with intentional 'breaks' for emphasis.
examples: The New York Times feature articles

entries.name: F-Pattern Layout
entries.summary: A content arrangement aligned to how users scan text-heavy pages: two horizontal sweeps across the top plus a vertical scan down the left edge, tracing an 'F' shape.
entries.significance: Documented by Nielsen Norman Group eye-tracking; explains why front-loaded headings, left-aligned key terms, and short leading text improve scannability of unformatted content.
key_facts: Originally discovered in a 2006 Nielsen Norman Group eye-tracking study of 232 users viewing thousands of pages.
Users read a top horizontal bar, a shorter second horizontal bar, then a vertical scan down the left side.
A follow-up NN/g study ~11 years later confirmed the original findings and noted it still applies on mobile.
The F-pattern emerges most on text-heavy pages lacking formatting; good hierarchy and layout can override it.

sources.title: F-Shaped Pattern For Reading Web Content (original eyetracking research)
F-Shaped Pattern of Reading on the Web: Misunderstood, But Still Relevant

entries.name: Z-Pattern Layout
entries.summary: A layout that guides the eye along a Z-shaped path: top-left to top-right, diagonally down to bott-left, then across to bottom-right, ending on a call-to-action.
entries.significance: Suits sparse, non-text-heavy pages (landing/marketing) by sequencing logo, nav, hero, and CTA along the natural scan route so the eye ends on the conversion point.
key_facts: Mirrors left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading with a diagonal sweep between the horizontals.
Best for pages that are not content-dense, unlike the text-heavy F-pattern.
Common sequence: brand top-left, nav/CTA top-right, feature diagonal, primary CTA bottom-right.
NN/g documents multiple scan patterns (F, spotted, layer-cake, commitment); Z is a design heuristic for light pages.

sources.title: F-Shaped Pattern of Reading on the Web (context on scan patterns)

entries.name: Single-Column (Long-Form) Layout
entries.summary: A single centered column of content with generous margins, optimized for uninterrupted linear reading of long-form text.
entries.significance: Maximizes readability and focus by controlling measure (line length) and removing sidebars; the default for articles, blogs, and documentation, and inherently mobile-friendly.
key_facts: Constrains the content column to a comfortable reading measure (commonly ~45-75 characters per line).
Removes competing sidebars so attention stays on the linear content flow.
Naturally responsive: a single column needs little rearrangement across breakpoints (see Tiny Tweaks).
Often paired with a sticky header or reading-progress indicator for navigation.

entries.name: Sidebar Layout
entries.summary: A two-column layout pairing a persistent navigation/utility sidebar with a wider main content area.
entries.significance: Standard for documentation, web apps, and settings screens where global navigation must stay visible; the sidebar anchors orientation while content scrolls.
key_facts: Usually a fixed- or min-width nav column beside a fluid main column (e.g. Grid: 250px 1fr).
Sidebar commonly becomes an off-canvas drawer on small screens.
The sidebar is often made sticky so navigation stays in view while the main column scrolls.
A dual-sidebar variant is the classic Holy Grail layout.

sources.title: Relationship of grid layout to other layout methods

entries.name: Dashboard / Admin Layout
entries.summary: An app-shell layout combining a persistent nav sidebar, a top bar, and a main region filled with modular widget cards (charts, tables, stats).
entries.significance: The canonical structure for data-dense back-office and analytics tools; the modular grid lets diverse widgets coexist and rearrange, while the shell keeps navigation and context fixed.
key_facts: Combines an app shell (persistent sidebar + top bar) with a widget grid main area.
Widgets are typically cards sized on a grid to imply relative importance (overlaps with bento layout).
Prioritizes information density and at-a-glance scanning over marketing polish.
Frequently uses CSS Grid for the widget region and Flexbox within each widget.

entries.name: Sticky Header
entries.summary: A header/navigation bar that remains fixed to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls, keeping brand and primary navigation always accessible.
entries.significance: Keeps navigation and key actions reachable without scrolling back up, reducing interaction cost on long pages; a near-universal pattern that trades a slice of vertical space for constant access.
key_facts: Implemented with CSS position: sticky (or fixed) so the bar detaches at a scroll threshold.
Consumes permanent viewport height, so it must be kept shallow (especially on mobile).
Often combined with hide-on-scroll-down / show-on-scroll-up behavior to reclaim space.
Requires scroll-margin/scroll-padding fixes so anchor jumps aren't hidden behind the bar.

entries.name: Sticky Sidebar
entries.summary: A sidebar (table of contents, filters, or ad rail) that scrolls with the page until it reaches a boundary, then sticks in view while the main content continues to scroll.
entries.significance: Keeps secondary navigation or context visible during long reads without a full fixed panel; common for in-page tables of contents and product filter rails.
key_facts: Built with position: sticky and a top offset, within a container taller than the sidebar.
Sticks only within its parent's bounds; it releases at the container's end.
Popular for article tables of contents that highlight the current section.
Fails silently if an ancestor has overflow:hidden or the parent isn't tall enough to allow sticking.

sources.title: Relationship of grid layout to other layout methods

entries.name: Off-Canvas / Hamburger Navigation
entries.summary: Navigation hidden off-screen behind a hamburger (or menu) icon that slides in as a drawer when toggled, reserving screen space for content.
entries.significance: Solves limited mobile width by hiding secondary navigation, but NN/g research shows hidden navigation measurably hurts discoverability and task performance, so it should be used deliberately.
key_facts: Content stays on-canvas; navigation lives off-screen and slides in via a toggle (JavaScript/CSS transform).
NN/g found hidden navigation cuts discoverability roughly in half versus visible navigation.
Hidden menus also increase task time and perceived task difficulty in NN/g studies.
The three-line 'hamburger' icon is now widely recognized but still carries these usability tradeoffs.
examples: responsive site headers under a breakpoint

sources.title: Hamburger Menus and Hidden Navigation Hurt UX Metrics

entries.name: Mega Menu
entries.summary: A large, two-dimensional dropdown panel that reveals many navigation options at once, grouped into labeled columns, on hover or click of a top-level nav item.
entries.significance: Lets users of large sites see and scan a whole category tree simultaneously instead of drilling through nested single-column dropdowns; favored by e-commerce and enterprise sites with deep hierarchies.
key_facts: Displays grouped links in a wide panel so options are scannable in parallel rather than sequentially.
Suited to sites with large, structured content hierarchies (retail, enterprise, universities).
On mobile, mega menus are typically converted to accordion-style expandable sections.
Grouping and clear labels are essential; an ungrouped wall of links overwhelms rather than helps.

entries.name: Bottom Tab Bar (Mobile)
entries.summary: A persistent horizontal bar of 3-5 primary destinations fixed to the bottom of the screen, used as top-level navigation in mobile apps and app-like web experiences.
entries.significance: Places core navigation in the thumb-reachable zone and keeps it always visible, avoiding the discoverability loss of hidden hamburger menus; the standard for native mobile apps.
key_facts: Positioned at the bottom of the screen for one-handed thumb reach.
Typically limited to 3-5 top-level destinations with icon + label.
Keeps navigation persistently visible, unlike off-canvas hamburger menus.
Codified in both iOS (Tab Bar) and Material Design (Navigation Bar) guidelines.

entries.name: Bottom Sheet
entries.summary: A surface that slides up from the bottom edge of the screen to present supplementary content or actions, either partially (peeking) or fully expanded, and dismissed by swiping down.
entries.significance: A mobile-native alternative to center modals that keeps interaction anchored in the thumb zone; supports progressive expansion so users see a preview before committing to the full surface.
key_facts: Animates up from the bottom edge; dismissed by swipe-down or tapping the scrim.
Two main types: modal (blocks the app behind a scrim) and standard/persistent (coexists with content).
Often supports a partially-expanded 'peek' state before full expansion.
Defined as a component in Material Design guidelines.

entries.name: Modal / Dialog
entries.summary: An overlay window that appears above the page, dims the background with a scrim, and requires the user to act on or dismiss it before returning to the underlying content.
entries.significance: Focuses attention on a single decision or task (confirmations, forms, media), but because it interrupts flow and traps focus it should be reserved for content that genuinely requires immediate attention.
key_facts: Modal variants block interaction with the page behind a scrim until dismissed; non-modal overlays do not.
Accessible modals must trap focus, be dismissible via Escape, and return focus to the trigger on close.
The native <dialog> element and its showModal() method provide built-in modal semantics.
Overuse creates interruption fatigue; reserve for confirmations, critical input, or focused media.

entries.name: Full-Bleed Imagery
entries.summary: Images or video that extend edge-to-edge across the full viewport width, breaking out of the content container's margins for an immersive effect.
entries.significance: Creates dramatic, immersive moments and strong visual anchors; borrowed from print's 'bleed' concept and used to punctuate long content or headline a hero.
key_facts: Term 'bleed' comes from print, where ink extends past the trim edge of the page.
On the web it means media spanning 100% of viewport width, ignoring the content column's max-width.
Commonly implemented via a full-width breakout technique inside an otherwise constrained layout grid.
Requires responsive art direction so focal points survive cropping across aspect ratios.

entries.name: Asymmetric Layout
entries.summary: A deliberately unbalanced arrangement where elements of different sizes and weights are placed off-center to create visual tension, movement, and clear focal points.
entries.significance: Escapes the static feel of perfectly centered layouts; used to convey energy and modernity and to steer the eye toward a dominant element, while still resting on an underlying grid.
key_facts: Balance is achieved through visual weight rather than mirror symmetry.
Typically built on a grid but placing content off the central axis.
Creates directional flow and dynamic tension that guides the eye.
Rooted in Swiss/International Typographic Style, which favored asymmetric grid composition.

entries.name: Broken Grid / Overlap Layout
entries.summary: A layout that establishes a grid then intentionally violates it, overlapping elements or letting content spill across gridlines to create depth and an editorial, hand-made feel.
entries.significance: Signals creativity and craft by subverting the expected grid; effective for portfolios and fashion/editorial sites where distinctiveness matters more than strict order.
key_facts: Requires a strong underlying grid first, so the 'breaks' read as intentional rather than accidental.
Overlapping layers use CSS Grid stacking (same grid cell) plus z-index for depth.
Text and images may cross column boundaries to create tension.
Higher risk of accessibility/readability issues, so contrast and reading order need care.

sources.title: Relationship of grid layout to other layout methods

entries.name: Feature Sections (Zig-Zag / Alternating)
entries.summary: A stack of feature blocks that alternate image and text sides down the page (image left/text right, then reversed), creating rhythm while explaining multiple features.
entries.significance: Standard for SaaS and product marketing pages: the alternation maintains visual interest across many features and keeps each feature paired clearly with its supporting visual.
key_facts: Each row pairs one feature's copy with a supporting image or screenshot.
Alternating the image side row-to-row creates a zig-zag rhythm and prevents monotony.
On mobile, rows collapse to a consistent stacked order (usually image then text).
The reversal is often achieved by toggling flex-direction or grid column order per row.

entries.name: Pricing Table
entries.summary: A row of side-by-side plan columns listing price, features, and a CTA per tier, usually with one tier visually highlighted as recommended.
entries.significance: The conversion-critical layout for subscription products; enabling quick tier comparison and steering choice via a highlighted 'most popular' plan directly affects revenue.
key_facts: Typically 3-4 plan columns aligned so features can be compared horizontally across tiers.
One tier is usually visually emphasized (badge, elevation, accent) to anchor choice.
Often includes a monthly/annual toggle that updates displayed prices.
Collapses to stacked cards on mobile, generally leading with the recommended tier.

entries.name: Comparison Table
entries.summary: A matrix with items as columns and attributes as rows (often with check/cross marks), letting users compare options feature-by-feature.
entries.significance: Supports decision-making by making differences scannable at a glance; essential for product/plan comparisons but demands careful responsive handling because wide tables break on small screens.
key_facts: Structured as a matrix: options across columns, attributes down rows.
Boolean features are commonly shown with check/cross icons for fast scanning.
A sticky header row and/or sticky first column keeps labels visible while scrolling large tables.
Responsive strategies include horizontal scroll, column priority, or a card-per-item transform on mobile.

entries.name: Testimonial Wall
entries.summary: A dense grid or masonry of customer quotes, often with avatars, names, and company logos, presenting social proof at scale.
entries.significance: Builds credibility through volume and specificity of praise; the wall format signals that many real people endorse the product, reinforcing conversion.
key_facts: Frequently laid out as a masonry/multi-column grid because testimonials vary in length.
Each card typically includes a quote, a name, an avatar, and a company/role for authenticity.
A specific attributed quote is more persuasive than an anonymous or generic one.
Often combined with a logo cloud to pair qualitative and quantitative social proof.

entries.name: Logo Cloud / Social Proof Bar
entries.summary: A horizontal band of recognizable customer or partner logos, usually monochrome and evenly spaced, signaling credibility by association.
entries.significance: Provides fast, low-effort trust: recognizable brand logos borrow their credibility for the host product, which is why 'trusted by' bars sit high on landing pages.
key_facts: Logos are typically desaturated/monochrome to unify disparate brand colors and reduce distraction.
Usually placed just below the hero as an early trust signal ('trusted by' / 'as seen in').
Even spacing and consistent optical sizing matter because logos have different intrinsic proportions.
Often animated as a slow marquee when there are more logos than fit in one row.

entries.name: Fat Footer
entries.summary: A large, multi-column footer that acts as a secondary sitemap, grouping links to products, resources, company info, legal pages, and social/contact details.
entries.significance: Catches users who scroll to the bottom looking for wayfinding or utility links; consolidates deep navigation that doesn't belong in the primary header, improving findability of secondary pages.
key_facts: Organized into labeled link columns functioning as a secondary site map.
Common groups: Product, Resources, Company, Legal, plus contact and social icons.
Serves users who habitually scroll to the footer to find utility or wayfinding links.
Frequently includes newsletter signup, language/region switchers, and copyright.

entries.name: Hero + Trust Bar
entries.summary: A thin strip directly beneath the hero that surfaces credibility signals such as customer count, ratings, awards, or a compact logo row.
entries.significance: Reinforces the hero's claim with immediate proof at the moment of highest attention, reducing skepticism before the visitor scrolls further.
key_facts: Placed immediately after the hero, in the high-attention above-the-fold zone.
Content types: user/customer counts, star ratings, awards, or a monochrome logo cloud.
Acts as a bridge between the aspirational hero message and concrete evidence.
Kept visually lightweight so it supports rather than competes with the hero CTA.

entries.name: Sticky CTA
entries.summary: A call-to-action button or bar that stays fixed in view (often bottom of the mobile screen or within a header) as the user scrolls, keeping the primary action always one tap away.
entries.significance: Reduces the effort to convert at any scroll depth by keeping the primary action persistently reachable; especially valuable on long mobile pages where the hero CTA scrolls out of view.
key_facts: Implemented with position: sticky/fixed so the action persists across scroll.
On mobile it commonly anchors to the bottom edge within the thumb zone.
Keeps a single primary action reachable at any scroll depth, unlike a one-time hero CTA.
Must be shallow and dismissible-aware so it doesn't obscure content or block the last screen row.
examples: 'Book now' sticky buttons on booking sites

entries.name: Progressive Disclosure / Accordion
entries.summary: A pattern that hides secondary detail behind expandable sections (accordions, 'show more' toggles), revealing complexity only as the user requests it.
entries.significance: Reduces cognitive load and visual clutter by deferring advanced or lengthy content; introduced by Jakob Nielsen in 1995 as a way to keep common tasks simple while keeping advanced options available.
key_facts: Progressive disclosure was introduced by Jakob Nielsen (1995) as an interaction design technique to reduce errors and cognitive load.
An accordion is a vertically stacked set of expandable headers that show/hide their content on click.
Shows only the information a user needs at a given moment, deferring the rest until requested.
Widely used for FAQs, help centers, mobile navigation, and 'advanced settings' panels.

sources.title: The art of progressive disclosure in web design

entries.name: Infinite Scroll
entries.summary: A pattern that automatically loads and appends more content as the user approaches the bottom, creating a continuous feed without explicit page breaks.
entries.significance: Minimizes interaction cost and boosts engagement for continuous, flat-hierarchy content (social feeds, media), but NN/g cautions it removes landmarks, harms orientation, and can suppress conversions.
key_facts: NN/g: infinite scrolling reduces interruptions and is best for continuously streaming, flat-hierarchy content.
It removes landmarks, making it hard for users to orient themselves or return to a spot.
It can cause inaction and lower conversions, the opposite of most business goals, per NN/g.
A 'Load More' button is often a better compromise, preserving control while avoiding full pagination.

sources.title: Infinite Scrolling: When to Use It, When to Avoid It
Infinite Scrolling Is Not for Every Website

entries.name: Pagination
entries.summary: Splitting a large content set into discrete numbered pages with previous/next and page controls, so users move through it in defined chunks.
entries.significance: Gives users a sense of scope, completion, and control (they can gauge remaining effort and return to a specific page); NN/g notes it suits goal-oriented tasks like e-commerce browsing better than infinite scroll.
key_facts: Provides a clear beginning and end, giving users a sense of completion and control (NN/g).
Lets users anticipate effort and deliberately choose whether to continue to the next page.
Preferred for goal-oriented browsing such as e-commerce product listings and search results.
Enables deep-linking and bookmarking of a specific page, which infinite scroll typically breaks.

sources.title: Infinite Scrolling: When to Use It, When to Avoid It

entries.name: Skeleton Loading Layout
entries.summary: Grey placeholder shapes that mirror the final layout's structure while content loads, previewing where text and images will appear.
entries.significance: Reduces perceived wait time by signaling progress and the coming layout, and avoids the jank of empty space or spinners; NN/g documents it as a perceived-performance technique.
key_facts: A skeleton screen is a placeholder shown while a page loads, per NN/g.
It reduces the perception of long loading time by hinting at the final layout.
Placeholder shapes echo real content positions (text bars, image blocks, avatars).
Often animated with a shimmer to signal active loading rather than a frozen state.

entries.name: Empty State
entries.summary: The designed view shown when a container has no content yet (a new inbox, empty list, or zero search results), using guidance and a clear next action instead of blank space.
entries.significance: Turns a dead-end into onboarding: NN/g notes well-designed empty states increase user confidence, improve learnability, and help users start key tasks, especially in complex or newly-set-up apps.
key_facts: Empty states are common during onboarding and initial use of complex, not-yet-configured apps (NN/g).
Well-designed empty states increase user confidence, improve learnability, and speed first tasks (NN/g).
Effective empty states explain why the area is empty and offer a clear primary next action.
Three contexts exist: first-use (no data yet), user-cleared (all done), and error/zero-results.
examples: empty inbox 'you're all caught up' screens

sources.title: Designing Empty States in Complex Applications: 3 Guidelines

entries.name: Onboarding Flow
entries.summary: A guided sequence (welcome screens, tooltips/coach marks, checklists, or a step-by-step setup) that introduces a new user to a product and its first key tasks.
entries.significance: Shapes activation and retention by reducing the initial learning curve; layers on progressive disclosure and empty-state guidance to get users to their first success quickly.
key_facts: Common formats: welcome carousels, coach-mark tooltips, setup checklists, and interactive walkthroughs.
Applies progressive disclosure so users aren't shown all features at once.
Ties closely to empty states, which double as first-run onboarding surfaces.
Best practice keeps steps skippable and focused on reaching a first meaningful outcome ('aha moment').

sources.title: Designing Empty States in Complex Applications: 3 Guidelines

entries.name: Mostly Fluid
entries.summary: A responsive pattern with a fluid multi-column grid that mainly adds margins on large screens and stacks columns on the narrowest screens, with minimal structural change in between.
entries.significance: One of Luke Wroblewski's five multi-device layout patterns (2012); the most common approach because a single max-width plus a stacking breakpoint handles most content sites with little effort.
key_facts: One of five patterns catalogued by Luke Wroblewski in 'Multi-Device Layout Patterns' (March 14, 2012).
Content reflows fluidly within a container capped by max-width; large screens gain wider margins.
At the narrowest sizes, columns stack vertically.
Often needs only a single breakpoint on the parent container, with children reflowing automatically.
examples: typical blog and content marketing sites

entries.name: Column Drop
entries.summary: A responsive pattern that starts multi-column and progressively 'drops' columns to stack them into a single column as the viewport narrows, while element sizes stay fairly consistent.
entries.significance: One of Wroblewski's five patterns; the intuitive default for turning a desktop multi-column layout into a mobile stack without redesigning the composition.
key_facts: One of five patterns from Luke Wroblewski's 'Multi-Device Layout Patterns' (2012).
Columns stack one at a time into a single column as width decreases.
Unlike Mostly Fluid, element sizes tend to stay consistent rather than scaling with the viewport.
Column stacking order becomes an important content-priority decision on mobile.
examples: three-column marketing pages that stack on mobile

entries.name: Layout Shifter
entries.summary: A responsive pattern that uses distinctly different layouts at different breakpoints, actively rearranging content rather than just reflowing or stacking it.
entries.significance: The most flexible and creative of Wroblewski's five patterns, and the most work: it does the most to adapt across screen sizes, enabling tailored compositions per device class.
key_facts: One of five patterns from Luke Wroblewski's 'Multi-Device Layout Patterns' (2012).
Uses genuinely different layouts for large, medium, and small screens.
Adapts the most across screen sizes and is where much innovative responsive design happens.
Requires the most effort because multiple distinct layouts must be designed and maintained.
examples: bespoke marketing sites with device-specific compositions

entries.name: Tiny Tweaks
entries.summary: A responsive pattern that keeps one layout and only makes minor adjustments (font size, image sizing, margins/padding) as the viewport changes.
entries.significance: The simplest of Wroblewski's five patterns; ideal for single-column, text-centric pages where no structural rearrangement is needed, only proportional refinement.
key_facts: One of five patterns from Luke Wroblewski's 'Multi-Device Layout Patterns' (2012).
Adjusts only small details such as font sizes, image layout, margins, and padding.
Keeps the same overall layout across all screen sizes.
Was the least common of the five, since few sites are simple enough single-column designs to use it alone.
examples: minimal single-column articles and landing pages

entries.name: Off Canvas (Responsive Pattern)
entries.summary: A responsive pattern that pushes less-frequently used content (typically navigation) off-screen on small viewports and reveals it on demand or when the screen widens.
entries.significance: One of Wroblewski's five patterns and the structural basis of the hamburger/drawer navigation; it conserves scarce small-screen space instead of stacking everything into a long scroll.
key_facts: One of five patterns from Luke Wroblewski's 'Multi-Device Layout Patterns' (2012).
Hides secondary content/navigation off-screen rather than stacking or shrinking it.
Hidden content is retrieved on user action (JavaScript-driven) or shown when the viewport is wide enough.
It is the responsive-layout basis for the off-canvas / hamburger navigation UI pattern.
examples: responsive sites hiding nav behind a hamburger on mobile
